analysis: The stronger heuristics prioritize early non‑dominated pruning, reuse of Monte Carlo samples, and vectorized incremental updates to sample coverage over brute‑force hypervolume recomputation. They tend to employ chunking and avoid redundant computations while coupling a greedy selection with lightweight local swap refinements. In contrast, weaker heuristics suffer from repeated full recalculations and less efficient data reuse, incurring unnecessary overhead.
experience: Focus on reducing candidate size via pruning, maximize vectorized operations and shared sample reuse, and limit full‐HV recalculations with local swap refinements. Keep memory usage controlled by chunking computations to boost overall runtime efficiency.
current_reflection: Focus on reducing candidate size via pruning, maximize vectorized operations and shared sample reuse, and limit full‐HV recalculations with local swap refinements. Keep memory usage controlled by chunking computations to boost overall runtime efficiency.
bad_reflections: Favor hybrid greedy+approximate HVC: pre-prune dominated points, estimate marginal contributions by vectorized Monte‑Carlo sampling (maintain a covered-samples bitmap), update contributions incrementally, and only run exact HV for final verification or occasional swaps. Cache computations, avoid recreating hypervolume objects in loops, and keep representations as boolean masks/arrays to minimize Python-level overhead.

Prune dominated points early, estimate marginal HV with a shared Monte‑Carlo sample set, keep a covered‑samples bitmap and update counts incrementally (vectorized/chunked), and limit exact HV calls to a small shortlist used for local swaps. Avoid per-candidate HV in inner loops, repeated object construction, and Python-level membership checks in hot paths.

Pre‑prune dominated points, reuse one shared sample set, compute a chunked boolean dominated matrix, and maintain incremental uncovered‑sample counts for greedy picks. Cap sample size adaptively, reserve exact HV only for a tiny shortlist, avoid per‑candidate HV recomputation, prefer vectorized numpy operations, and add deterministic seeds and simple box‑volume tie‑breaks.
comprehensive_reflection:
• Keywords: Candidate pruning, vectorization, shared sample reuse, local swaps, computation chunking.  
• Advice: Prune the candidate set early, reuse shared samples for estimates, and employ efficient vectorized operations.  
• Avoid: Full hypervolume recalculations in inner loops and bloated memory usage from non-chunked processing.  
• Explanation: Limiting expensive operations to local swaps and processing data in manageable chunks balances speed and precision, ultimately boosting runtime efficiency.
